envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Administration of Air and Water Resource and Solid Waste Management Programs

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The classified establishments exercise statutory regulatory and administrative authority funded through appropriations, taxes, grants, or fees. Regulated entities and the public are stakeholders rather than arm's-length outsourced-service customers, and agency budgets or public benefits are not transferable EBITDA.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default lens produces an empty population because Census defines this industry as government establishments administering environmental programs. There is no coherent narrowing that yields a transferable private firm without moving to a different NAICS code.
lens.excluded_activities: All federal, state, local, and tribal government environmental-program establishments are excluded from the transferable-firm population because they are not privately controlled commercial firms. Private environmental consulting, engineering, laboratory, software, water and sewage operations, waste collection, treatment, disposal, and remediation providers are classified outside NAICS 924110.
lens.included_activities: No privately owned firm satisfies the fixed lens. For operational context only, classified government activities include administration, regulation, enforcement, and intergovernmental coordination of air and water resources, pollution control and prevention, solid and toxic waste, flood control, drainage development, water consumption, and cleanup programs.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across all government ownership and separately describes environmental-scientist work, but it does not publish a wage-weighted task mix for NAICS 924110. I map portions of permitting, records, reporting, data validation, monitoring analysis, scientific synthesis, modeling, inspections triage, complaint intake, grant administration, research, drafting, and public communication to not-yet-automated AI exposure, while reserving field sampling, investigation, legal judgment, negotiation, enforcement, and accountable decisions for people. The proxy may overstate substitution in evidentiary work or understate opportunity in large environmental datasets.
caveats: No occupation dataset isolates NAICS 924110, making the wage-weighted exposure a proxy.
The assigned labor-share input is missing because government agencies lack comparable receipts.
Exposure describes government work even though the fixed commercial lens has no eligible firms.
Existing monitoring networks, rules engines, GIS tools, and statistical models must not be counted again as new AI opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory for federal, state, local, and tribal NAICS 924110 establishments that identifies existing automation and separates permitting, science, field, enforcement, legal, grant, and administrative work would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The industry combines office and administrative processing, science and engineering analysis, legal and policy work, compliance monitoring, and some field inspection. Environmental scientists explicitly collect, synthesize, study, report, and recommend action from air, soil, water, food, and other measurements. AI can assist document review, permit completeness checks, data quality, anomaly detection, remote-sensing interpretation, modeling, prioritization, research, drafting, and public-service workflows, but cannot replace all field evidence, statutory judgment, or enforcement accountability.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for federal government and state and local government excluding education and hospitals, not environmental-program administration specifically. Their 0.4 percent and 0.6 percent annual growth from 2024 to 2034 imply five-year ratios near 1.02 and 1.03. I center slightly above that broad proxy because environmental statutes sustain permitting, monitoring, reporting, enforcement, cleanup, and coordination across a regulated universe that EPA's ECHO exporter says exceeds 1.5 million facilities. The proxy may overstate demand if policy retrenchment or automation reduces workload, or understate it if new pollutants, infrastructure, disasters, and data requirements expand it.
caveats: BLS output aggregates are much broader than NAICS 924110.
EPA facility and enforcement figures do not cover all state, local, tribal, flood-control, drainage, or water-consumption programs.
The ECHO facility universe is not a direct count of annual work units and can include overlapping program identifiers.
Legislation and administrative policy can change environmental workload abruptly.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-quality workload index across all government levels covering permits, regulated facilities, monitoring records, inspections, enforcement cases, cleanup sites, grants, flood and water-resource actions, and intergovernmental coordination would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, hazardous-waste, drinking-water, cleanup, pollution-prevention, and related mandates create recurring administration. EPA data systems cover a very large regulated universe, and 2025 civil enforcement activity remained substantial. Policy changes, court decisions, staffing, delegated state programs, infrastructure investment, extreme events, new contaminants, and automation can move constant-quality workload in either direction.

caveats: The assigned LMM firm-count input is missing, consistent with the government and SUSB scope exclusion.
Zero is a structural lens conclusion rather than an imputation for missing data.
Adjacent environmental-services markets may contain acquisition candidates but are outside this record.
e.change_evidence: A Census classification revision explicitly permitting privately controlled commercial establishments within NAICS 924110 would be required to move this value.
e.rationale: Census defines NAICS 924110 as government establishments administering and regulating environmental programs. Private consultants, engineers, labs, software vendors, utilities, waste operators, and remediation contractors may serve these agencies or regulated entities, but they are classified in other industries and cannot be treated as firms inside this code.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($12,168,745,231) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: A high operator-required share does not imply current staffing, jurisdictional allocation, or workflows remain unchanged.
Delegated state and tribal programs and private contractors can change which public body performs work while public accountability persists.
This describes durable public administration, not an investable private operator population.
o.change_evidence: Statutory changes that remove government responsibility for these programs, or evidence that private software and contractors legally replace rather than support the accountable agency, would materially lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Environmental statutes delegate rulemaking, permits, program administration, enforcement, grants, and intergovernmental coordination to accountable government bodies. Contractors and software can conduct studies, operate systems, process data, or support inspections, but cannot independently exercise sovereign regulatory authority or bear final public accountability. AI therefore changes execution more than the requirement for a classified government operator.

caveats: Fees and appropriations do not create an arm's-length commercial value-capture mechanism.
Public benefits and regulatory outcomes are not equivalent to retained gross profit.
q.change_evidence: A privately owned operator population with observable customer pricing, contracts, repricing, and retained margins would be required to estimate q.
q.rationale: Commercial retention after contractual pass-through, renewal repricing, or competition is undefined because the establishments do not sell an outsourced service for private-owner profit. AI benefits may appear as faster permits, better monitoring, more inspections, reduced backlog, avoided public cost, or reassigned appropriations rather than retained commercial margin.

caveats: The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a.
EPA capability may not transfer to smaller state, local, and tribal programs.
An inventoried use case or internal tool deployment does not prove scaled labor substitution.
AI recommendations may require scientific, legal, and supervisory review.
rho.change_evidence: Audited production results from environmental agencies showing adoption, accuracy, appeals, false positives, staff hours avoided, permit cycle times, inspection yield, enforcement outcomes, and sustained use by workflow would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: EPA maintains an AI use-case inventory and deployed a secure agency-wide generative-AI tool in 2025, demonstrating institutional capability. Implementation across five years remains constrained by fragmented federal, state, local, tribal, and legacy systems; incomplete or inconsistent environmental data; procurement; scientific validation; privacy and confidential-business information; cybersecurity; administrative procedure; records and disclosure duties; explainability; environmental justice; evidentiary standards; and human accountability for permits and enforcement.

caveats: Government restructuring is not a qualifying control transfer.
Procurement of a contractor does not transfer control of the regulatory agency.
s5.change_evidence: This primitive would become estimable only if privately controlled commercial firms entered the industry definition and an observable transaction population existed.
s5.rationale: A qualifying private control-transfer probability is undefined because there are no eligible firms. Elections, appointments, agency reorganizations, delegated program authority, intergovernmental transfers, and outsourcing contracts are not acquisitions of privately controlled operating companies in NAICS 924110.

principal_driver: Large, recurring statutory permitting, monitoring, and enforcement workloads create information-rich processes that AI can assist while public authority remains necessary.
principal_weakness: The code contains government regulators rather than transferable commercial operators, leaving firm availability and commercial value capture absent or undefined.
